experiment: #94 real-SIMD kernel microbench: fused TQ2P/TQ3P (share q8_K activation across planes) vs #94 route (K stock TQ2_0 mul_mats summed, re-quantize activation K times)
method: ggml_mul_mat on the patched llama.cpp@b9509 build, RTX 3090 pod CPU (128 cores), random packed bytes (timing data-independent for these branch-free AVX2 dot kernels), decode M=1
conclusion: The #94 route (K stock TQ2_0 tensors summed on the graph) is within ~1.00-1.15x of the fused kernel at real AVX2. Single-thread decode is ~1.00-1.02x (weight-memory-bound: both read the same total plane bytes; sharing the tiny q8_K activation across planes is negligible); ~1.13x at 8 threads. Portability (mainline, no TQ2P/TQ3P patch) costs little speed. STRONGER/cleaner than the earlier scalar microbench (~1.1-1.2x).
caveats: Measures per-layer mul_mat throughput, NOT a full end-to-end model run (the graph-sum in build_graph + a K-plane loader are still unimplemented; the format guarantees correctness, this measures the speed cost).
Random packed bytes (valid: these AVX2 kernels are branch-free, timing is data-independent).
nth=1 is the cleanest decode proxy; real decode uses a thread pool so the true penalty is ~1.0-1.15x.
